comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: I’d like to find a book that shows the classical attributions given to the planets as well as the types of offerings/libations one can offer. Which book(s) do you suggest?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'm going to assume you've already learned the basics of classical planetary magic -- the sort of thing you'd learn from Chris Warnock's books and website, and in the translation of Picatrix Chris and I did. If you want to go beyond that, you're facing serious research in primary and secondary scholarly sources, as I don't know of any book for practitioners that covers the subject in the kind of detail you have in mind. The Greek and Roman astrological authors are one starting place; another is Pausanius' Description of Greece, which dates from long after Greek religion took on its postclassical astrological dimension; another angle begins from Hans Lewy's Chaldean Oracles and Theurgy and Iamblichus' On the Mysteries -- but each of these are starting points, and you'll be chasing sources from there into odd corners of classical literature and the scholarly work based on them. You might, if you decide to pursue this, consider writing a book yourself that sums up your discoveries, since this is a subject practitioners would no doubt like to learn more about!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you so much.

I had not thought about writing a book either. Thanks for the inspiration.

comments.title: John Crowley
comments.content:
I just listened to a podcast of last year where you mentioned enthusiastically the books of John Crowley especially his Aegypt cycle.

Can you tell us a bit more about why you really liked his books?

Regards

Karim

replies.title: Re: John Crowley
replies.content: I'm never sure how to respond to a question like this. Um, I like Crowley's books because I find reading them enjoyable -- but does that tell you anything you didn't already know? Still, that's basically the situation. I don't enjoy a book for this or that reason; I enjoy a book, and then maybe I can come up with some sort of clumsy attempt at explanation for why I enjoy it.

replies.title: Re: John Crowley
replies.content: OK! Understandable....

comments.title: Magical trees and bushes
comments.content:
Dear JMG and company of mages, can you suggest appropriate magical trees and bushes for my yard? I bought a little house a year ago in the Midwestern US with little to no restrictions concerning what I do with my yard, which is 1/6 of an acre in Zone 5 with full sun. It's not huge, however, the house is small enough that it does not occupy a great deal of the yard. I have planted a dwarf pear tree on the south side and a Blue Princess holly bush in the East. Apparently the Princess needs a male consort, so I'll buy her a mate soon. I was going to plant some apple trees I sprouted from Aldi apples out there, but they almost died so they are inside now, recovering. Other than some raised beds, it's a blank slate. There will be two rain gardens out of necessity.

I have been studying Ogham, but I am a slow learner and have not yet made a set of staves. Should I be planting hawthorn? Birch? Hazelnut? What would you plant? Thanks in advance.

replies.title: Re: Magical trees and bushes
replies.content: A great deal depends on what exactly you want to do -- what energies you want to attract to your yard, what effects you want to bring about. I'd encourage you to get a good basic book on natural magic -- my Encyclopedia of Natural Magic is one of the available choices -- and go through the trees and other plants listed in the book, asking yourself which of them have energies you want to bring into your life.

replies.title: Re: Magical trees and bushes
replies.content: That makes sense.  I do own that one already and am plodding my way through.  I have a bunch of vegetables I'm waiting until next week to plant because the moon will finally be waxing again and going through the sign of Cancer.  Hope this is correct.  Thanks for the advice.

replies.title: Re: Magical trees and bushes
replies.content: Gemini during a waxing moon is good for fruit trees, as per _Planetary Planting_ by Louise Ricotte (who also wrote on companion planting).  Libra is good for flowers and herbs, also during a waxing moon.  Cancer, Pisces, and Scorpio are good for planting in general, above ground crops during the waxing and below ground crops during the waning moon.  (I spent last year learning this!  Now, it's my turn to share.)

replies.title: Re: Magical trees and bushes
replies.content: Thank you!  Great advice.

replies.title: Re: Magical trees and bushes
replies.content: There's a big difference between growing magical stock and creating a space for spirits to inhabit. I would seriously take a look at the landscape around you. What's within walking distance and what's already going on there?

replies.title: Re: Magical trees and bushes
replies.content: Yes, that's a difference I don't know much about. There are two cemeteries within a short walk from my house and several more nearby.  The rest is old houses in various stages of (dis)repair.  The tallest trees are the stately white oaks.  Black walnuts are native to my area.

replies.title: Re: Magical trees and bushes
replies.content: Try to get hold of books by Diana Beresford Kroeger, especially "Arboretum America" and "A Garden for Life"  http://www.stuartbernstein.com/dianaberesford-kroeger.html.  Diana was raised in Ireland by Gaelic grandparents who didn't speak English.  She knows much about the traditional Celtic relationship to trees and tells those stories beautifully.  She will tell you why you should never bring certain plants indoors(because some have such strong medicine).  Another author I recently discovered is Robin Wall Kimmerer, who is Native American and covers similar territory from her point of view.  Her books are "Braiding Sweet Grass", and "Gathering Moss". Both women are professional research academics in the medical and biological sciences respectively,  and their work combines their respect for the elders' wisdom and current research into plant biology and environmental remediation.  A third author is the late Sara Stein, check out her books "Noah's Garden" and planting "Noah's Garden.  Stein's books are more about planting your yard to provide an "ark" for the pollinators, birds and small mammals who are losing so much habitat to development and climate change.  Compared to typical landscaping or gardening books, these authors' writings provide a completely different way to think about what plants you might want in your life.

replies.title: Re: Magical trees and bushes
replies.content: Thank you so much for this!  Having lived in situations where I had little to no control over the outdoor space, for years I longed to create a food garden for myself and the creatures that inhabit this corner of the world.  I hate lawns.  These book recommendations are perfect.

replies.title: Re: Magical trees and bushes
replies.content:
Kimberley -
Apples grown from seed don't produce the same kind of apple that they came from, due to genetic mixing and cross-pollination. The apples that we like to eat (as opposed to pressing for cider) have been carefully selected and then propagated by grafting to preserve their unique genetics. (Johnny Appleseed was selling apple trees for making hard cider, by the way, a detail glossed over by Disney.)

Lathechuck

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: A commenter last week asked about doing divination in a banished space, which you didn't discourage, but I remain curious as to what effect a banishing ritual would be expected to have on subsequent divinations performed there. I have been wont to do my divination in the same room as the SoP, although most often not in the precise area enclosed by thel sphere, and have gotten good resuts. I think I recall you saying that a banishing ritual essentially clears away etheric (or was it astral?) crud, but I am not very clear about the distinctions between the planes. Do you have any knowledge/speculation on the mechanism by which Ogham divination works, and if it operates on a particular plane? Does a banishing ritual "chase away" whatever makes divination work, or allow it to come through more clearly, or have some other effect entirely?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: My suggestion that people not do their divination in a banished space is mostly to encourage people not to get dependent on having a banished space in which to do divination -- it's convenient to be able to cast a reading when you want one, without going through the process of banishing the space first. That's all that's involved here.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Ah. Well, I was certainly making that more complicated than necessary. Thank you for letting me know!

comments.title: Return to Sender amulets
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,
I learned to make return to sender amulets from your Encyclopedia of Natural Magic. I wanted to make the amulet stronger so in addition to the salt and the bent nail, I added a piece of quartz, a pinch of blue vervain and three deadly nightshade berries. Is it possible that this amulet is too strong, that it might amplify ill wishes as it returns them? This is not my intention.

I have made these for sensate/empathic friends and they like them and feel they are getting good results. I wear mine all the time as our community is somewhat afflicted with vicious gossip.

Thanks you for all the interesting articles and books.
Max Rogers

replies.title: Re: Return to Sender amulets
replies.content: No, that shouldn't amplify anything, but it would make for a very solid protective amulet. Nicely done.

comments.title: Questions
comments.content:
Hello JMG,
Do you have a favourite tarot deck, or perhaps a top 5?
Do you use different decks for different circumstances?
Is it worth reading any Crowley or are there other authors that discuss his method that you would recommend?
I am just starting chapter 3 of your ritual magic book, and I definitely have a mind like a vagabond, it wanders all over the place!
I am determined to stick with it, I keep thinking of your mantra of Keep Going.

Last question, do you have a spell to help find small, misplaced objects?
Thanks in advance
Helen

replies.title: Re: Questions
replies.content:
1) I originally learned on the Aquarian Tarot by David Palladini, and still enjoy using it. Jean Beauchard's Tarot Symbolique Maconnique (Masonic Symbolic Tarot) and RJ Stewart & Miranda Gray's Merlin Tarot are two of my other favorites. I've done a lot of work with Robert Wang's Golden Dawn Tarot but it's pretty ugly!

2) I don't do much tarot divination these days. Horary astrology is my go-to method for divination right now.

3) I don't recommend Crowley at all, nor do I recommend his methods. To my mind, he's the poster child for what not to do if you're practicing magic.

4) No, but some people find they can use geomantic divination to do this; the instructions are in my book The Art and Practice of Geomancy.

replies.title: Re: Questions
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender)

When the object belongs to me, I have had some success by asking the object to tell me where it is.

There is a divinatory card deck that is more than two hundred years old and is unrelated to the Tarot. It is called Madame Lenormand's. Lenormand is entirely oriented toward answering practical questions, not spiritual development. It has different symbols from Tarot, so to learn it you need both a deck (though in a pinch you can use an ordinary playing card deck with the deuces through the fives removed) and an explanatory book. The best book, according to two experienced readers I asked, is Rana George's The Essential Lenormand. I'm a beginner with Lenormand and a fairly experienced Tarot reader, and I like it.

Rana George's book contains detailed instructions for locating lost objects with the cards. The method uses association of the symbols on the cards with particular objects and functions in the environment where the lost item is likely to be. For example, if you draw the "Bouquet" card, look near places where cosmetics, paints and colored pencils, or flower pots are kept. If you draw "Snake", look in drains and where there are pipes, wires, tubes, ropes and things that wind or coil or can make an "S" shape.

If you happen to have on hand another illustrated divinatory deck, you could try using this associative method with it, but it's easier with the Lenormand deck because there are only one or two symbols on each card. If you don't have any picture deck at all, but you have a picture book with varied illustrations that include indoor and outdoor scenes, you could try bibliomancy.

I don't use a pendulum for divination, but people who do would use the pendulum over a map or a diagram of the house or the room where the object is likely to be.

comments.title: (no subject)

replies.title: (no subject)

comments.title: Spirit above clockwise direction
comments.content:
Hello, a simple question today.
As I'm doing the SOP, I always have a moment's doubt drawing the circle above my head when invoking Spirit Above. To make it properly clockwise, should I imagine a clock face above me facing downwards (towards me), or should I imagine it facing upwards (towards the sky). I usually do it as if the clock were facing me (which is consistent with all the other moves in the SOP), but sometimes I wonder if all the clocks have a fixed reference which is the earth, in which case I'd be looking at the "back" of the clock from below.

replies.title: Re: Spirit above clockwise direction
replies.content: Nope -- it's always clockwise as seen from your point of view. The fixed reference is always the person who's doing the ritual.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: You've said you don't find the concept of evil useful (I forget if that was your exact phrase but it definitely wasn't positive). I thought the same for a long time but then I discovered the theory of wetiko and it made a lot of sense to me. How do you think of the acts and people generally considered evil? Especially those in Terry Eagleton's generously narrow definition of evil - torturing your enemies for information, enslaving them or killing them quickly and efficiently don't count as they may serve an amoral but rational goal. Only making people suffer for no reason and no benefit to yourself, or even being willing to damage your own position in order to hurt someone else count as true evil.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Why is it useful to have a single abstract label such as "evil" to put on those actions?

comments.title: Reverse one’s own evil spell?
comments.content:
For a many years, an enemy has treated me and mine with extraordinarily pertinacious malice, notable for its irrationality. Several years ago, after a series of most unpleasant episodes provoked solely by my enemy, I was ready for some solid revenge. I struck with malefic magic.

In retrospect, this wasn’t at all a good idea. I doubt it’s ever right to do malefic; if there are any circumstances that can justify it, these weren’t them. And of course the consequences fell on myself.

It was a lunar Mansion; not the worst, but pretty nasty. A talisman was involved; I destroyed it.

Is there anything further I can do to reverse the effects of the magic, both with regard to myself, and the object of my attack? If so, how ought I to proceed?

I doubt I can ever reconcile with this individual, but perhaps I should do something to diminish the hatred between us. There’s even a Mansion for that; though I’m not so keen on Mansions as before.

replies.title: Re: Reverse one’s own evil spell?
replies.content:
Start with divination. Always use divination to figure out whether a working would be a good idea. Especially in a case like this, you need to get some sense of what will work to untie the emotional-karmic knot that connects you and your enemy.

Depending on what the divination says, you have a range of options. It may be appropriate for you to do a working to lessen the hatred between the two of you. It may be appropriate for you to do a working to cut the connection between the two of you. It may be appropriate for you to do a working to bless your enemy. It may not be appropriate for you to do any further magic at all. There really is no one-size-fits-all rule.

replies.title: Re: Reverse one’s own evil spell?
replies.content: Thank you! I will proceed with divination as you advise, and act -or not - accordingly.

comments.title: Aurum Solis
comments.content:
I posted this question on the main blog but I guess I was too late.

Do you know anything about the origins of the Aurum Solis and if it was truly an independent magical system developed separately from the Golden Dawn?

For that matter, how much experience would you say someone should have before trying to experiment with either creating his own system or modifying an existing one? I don't have too much knowledge of the early origins of the GD, but it seems to me that although the founders were in contact with some practitioners of crystal magic and talismans (Hockley et al) they themselves never got very deep into the Renaissance methods before developing their own system.

Thanks
Alvin

replies.title: Re: Aurum Solis
replies.content:
The Aurum Solis does seem to have been an independent organization, but the techniques presented in the published books on the system are massively influenced by the Golden Dawn.

As for the origins of the Golden Dawn, au contraire -- W.W. Westcott in particular was a student of Renaissance and early modern esotericism for decades before he helped found the Golden Dawn; the Collectanea Hermetica series of reprints he edited covered an impressive range of alchemical, Hermetic, and occult literature. I'm also fairly sure that the whole "Fraulein Sprengel" subterfuge -- the imaginary German adept who supposedly gave them the charter for the GD -- was a cover story meant to protect the actual sources of the system, which were English rather than German and likely involved individuals whose involvement in occultism couldn't be made public -- quite possibly because one or more of the people involved were Anglican clergymen. (I'm still piecing together the evidence for this, and will publish it when I have everything assembled.)

Before you create a new system or modify an existing one, it's necessary to learn at least one system of magic completely, top to bottom, exactly as given. The notion that you can invent a system of magic when you've never learned one is right up there with claiming that you can invent workable recipes when you've never learned how to cook.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Please can you point me to one of your books or blog posts which introduces the planes?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I haven't written one yet.

comments.title: On offerings
comments.content:
Dear Mr. Greer,

This may not strictly be an occult question.

When one makes an offering of a specific object to a spirit/god, is this establishing a relationship of reciprocal exchange, the importance of the offering matching or exceeding the magnitude of the boon required? If that is the case, does that not make one's motives for engaging the being purely selfish ones?
I ask because I find that part of me wants to praise and wish the being i am trying to engage to be well; whereas another part clearly wants something in return.
Is this a problem?

lordyburd

replies.title: Re: On offerings
replies.content: No human being is capable of doing anything that's completely unselfish. The gods are aware of this -- after all, they made us that way! Prayer is the building of a relationship, and relationships always have an element of exchange; be honest with yourself and with the god or spirit you're invoking, and let both sides of you -- the part that wants to praise and bless, and the part that wants to ask for help -- participate fully in the offering and the prayer, and you'll be fine. The gods do not expect you to be something you aren't, and while they'll guide you to become more than you are right now, they'll do it step by step, in their own way.

comments.title: Re Television
comments.content: I have been fascinated by the discussion in a previous post on the possible natural magic remedy for the pull many people feel, against their will, towards television. I am familar with some of the esoteric ideas regarding technology, eg., Dr John Lilly (solid state entity), Dmitri Orlov (the technosphere) and Rudolph Steiner (Ahriman), but I am very curious to know your idea about what might be the source of any malign enchantment promoting television watching.

replies.title: Re: Re Television
replies.content: I don't know -- yet.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What do you think about invasive species of plants, in regards to magic?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: The entire concept of "invasive species" is based on bad science and a false concept of nature as a static reality. All species are invasive; the organisms that you see around you, wherever you are, either got there from somewhere else or evolved in place and elbowed other species out of the way. Nature is always changing; species are always expanding their ranges, being pushed out of existing habitats, or both. Thus "invasive species" are just...species. Their magical role depends not on whether our (highly invasive!) species labels them as "invasive," but on their own magical properties, whatever those might be.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: What I find funny is that people seem to refuse to use highly beneficial invasives for things that their native culture uses them for as a matter of course. It's an odd blind spot...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hey JMG
When doing middle pillar exercise do you imagine each chakra/sephiroth inside your bady at each point, like an organ, or do you imagine them outside your body close to each point?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Inside the body. The Middle Pillar runs down the midline of the body, in front of the spinal column.

comments.title: Geomancy
comments.content:
Hello,

Does geomancy only work for questions with binary answers? I've been reading various things (including your book) on geomancy, and I'm not sure if the open-ended question I have in mind is appropriate. The question in mind: Where should I move?

I feel strongly that I must move to a different state but am thoroughly perplexed as to which one. And it seems asking an open-ended question doesn't make any sense in a geomancy context.

Thank you for your help.

replies.title: Re: Geomancy
replies.content:
Geomancy works best for yes-no questions, so it's not much use in the kind of divination you have in mind.

May I offer some advice, as one who's moved from state to state very often? Start with nonmagical methods. Come up with a list of the things you want to find in the place where you move, and then use internet research to find places in other states that have those things. Read the online newspapers from the places that look best to you, get a sense of the local culture and history, see which ones seem to fit your needs best. Then go for a visit to the one that looks best, and see what you think. That's when you want to bring divination into the picture -- when you've already engaged other methods of knowing, and want to add that extra fillip of information that comes from divining.

comments.title: Etheric connections
comments.content:
Well, I don't own a TV but I tried taping an open safety pin to the back of my mobile phone. I was seriously expecting to be a naysayer and give you the honest feedback that it just didn't work for me... except that it worked for me. So far, at least. I'll be interested to see if all of us reporting positive results still are doing so a month from now. If so, well... if this is just a placebo at work, more placebos, please!

Two questions for now:

(1) Based on your understanding of the theory of etheric energy, is there a place on your body that you recommend over others as a place to keep a sharp bit of iron for protection against TVs, etc in the wild? Is on the head better than over the heart, say?

(2) By carrying around bits of sharp cold iron, are there any "positive" etheric energies we could otherwise be benefiting from which we'd be cutting ourselves off from?

replies.title: Re: Etheric connections
replies.content:
1) In front of the solar plexus is the most important place to guard, since that's the etheric organ that draws in energies. If you wear a hat, having a second one in front of the third eye (between the eyebrows) would also be useful

2) Don't have the cold sharp iron in place when you're planning on going to a ball game or a rock concert, or getting ready for a romantic encounter. You should probably leave it off when going to religious services, too. All those are partly mediated by etheric forces.

replies.title: Re: Etheric connections
replies.content: Re: 2) Hmm.  Would that make keeping cold sharp iron on the body a potentially useful tool when dealing with tainted egregores (say, to minimize the problems from participating in such an organization's rituals)?

replies.title: Re: Etheric connections
replies.content: Only to a very limited degree. Sharp iron only wards off etheric influence; an organization with a tainted egregor will influence you on the mental and astral levels as well, and against that iron has no influence. Your best bet in such a situation is always to walk away.

replies.title: Re: Etheric connections
replies.content: Heh.  I suspect I learned that the hard way in a past incarnation; my extreme wariness of submerging myself in a crowd comes from somewhere!  I was thinking more of cold iron as a possible stopgap/precautionary measure for situations that demand it; it sounds like the answer is "not much, but better than nothing".  (Wasn't particularly clear in my first question; sorry about that.)

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

This might be an odd question, and apologise in advance, but I wanted to ask you about good astrologers? I have tried several, and besides being expensive it seems they never seem to get me or my chart or whatever.

Lately I’ve started to “feel” I need to move countries and although it has happened before nowadays things are much more complicated in my life. So at the very minimum I’d like to get some
Country- options checked beforehand.

Also, if I may ask, I missed the Book Club in your other website, although I have read quite a few discussions. What would be your suggestion for a Book or books for a complete newbie to start learning magic ? BTW is it suitable for anybody or are there some contraindications?

Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
My advice with regard to astrology, if you haven't been able to find an astrologer whose work resonates with you, is to learn how to interpret your own chart. It's not that difficult! There are many books on the subject; I'd encourage you to visit your local public library and start reading what you can find, and go from there. Don't worry about finding the "right" book; your birth chart is the most important book, and you'll learn most by reading a series of books and looking up your planetary placements and aspects in each of them.

As for magic, if you have a heart condition or are under a physician's care for any serious mental illness, you should probably stay away from it. Otherwise, the choice of basic books depend on whether you're comfortable with Judeo-Christian symbolism. If so, my book Learning Ritual Magic is a good introduction; if not, and you can handle Druidry, you might consider my book The Druid Magic Handbook.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: The Atlantic published an article about The Ritual Design Lab, which is "working to generate new rituals for modern life, with an eye to user experience." Have you heard of this already and what do you think of it?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I hadn't heard of it. From the article, at least, it sounds like they're rehashing the sort of Ritual Lite that was all the rage in the human potential movement in the '70s and the men's movement in the '90s: bland, harmless, entertaining little games for those who don't want anything serious. I could certainly be wrong, but that's what it looks like to me.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Worth keeping an eye on, though probably less as something in and of itself and more as an example of a trend. I've heard of similar things out of the LessWrong rationalists (especially the ones tied into Silicon Valley), though in that case it seems to have of a combination of the shards of the 1960s counterculture and a desire to replicate some of the features of religion they like (i.e, not throw out the baby with the bathwater - the original ratsphere had heavy overlap with the New Atheists).

(I suspect the LW rats will wind up being the important group, though I'm not sure whether that will be in a thought-leaders-in-the-corridors-of-power sense or as a downwardly-mobile nucleus of new occult and/or religious ideas. Possibly both, if and when they splinter. At any rate, they're one of the few groups I've heard of currently building intentional communities*, they're playing with ideas that seem to correspond to effects in traditional occultism**, and I'm suspicious that part of the reason for the Effective Altruists' weirdness above and beyond the usual transhumanist stuff is that they're tapping into an archetype.)

* - We'll see how well that holds. AFAICT they're trying to route around the usual sex-and-jealousy issues with some of the modern polygamy experiments (primary/secondary partners, some ethics taken from the BDSM scene); it might even work, as long as they can select strongly enough for specific neurotypes. That said, I heard rattlings of Drama (tm) not even a week ago...
** - Speaking of that: if you can get access without paying an arm and a leg, you might nose around some of the newer stuff coming out of the predictive processing neuroscience and AI researchers. Some of it sounds suspiciously like grounding for divination, and the kind of research talked about in this tumblr post might be of interest for any parapsychologist interested in aura vision.

comments.title: Enochian Magic
comments.content:
Studying traditional western occultism, I've been seeing some references to Enochian Magic. The little I have read concerning it has piqued my interest. For what it's worth my interest is entirely academic, this sort of practice being way out of my depth. That being said the narrative around the discovery of the angelic language and its uses is utterly fascinating. Of course, there are many referenced to Enochian magic as an especially dangerous form of practice. On the wikipedia page https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Enochian_magic, Dion Fortune is quoted as saying:

“I have personal knowledge of more than twenty-five instances where the performance of [Enochian] magical operations based upon the Order’s [i.e. Alpha et Omega’s] formulae led to serious disintegrations of mind and body… Perhaps the most conspicuous example of the use of these formulas is A.C. [Aleister Crowley] himself, but there are plenty of others I have personally witnessed, whose personal shipwrecks have been just as complete even though their smaller tonnage, so to say, makes the loss seem less deplorable...”

My questions are: is it your experience that Enochian magic is especially dangerous and if so why? What about the practice causes these "disintegrations of mind and body"? In my possession, I have your edited version of Israel Regardie's The Golden Dawn which as yet I've only flipped through and ordered your New Encyclopedia of the Occult from Llewellyn recently. Are there other books that you would recommend to better understand the history and development of Enochian magic?

replies.title: Re: Enochian Magic
replies.content:
Enochian magic is dangerous because it's both extremely powerful and extremely specific. Each force invoked from the Enochian tablets is highly focused and relatively pure, and so has to be handled with extreme care. In the classic Golden Dawn system, you get to Enochian working only after you've trained intensively with the more general forces of Cabalistic magic, so you know what you're doing and the magic doesn't blow up in your face.

Crowley's problem, which is very widely shared in today's occult scene, was that he believed he was a lot more competent than he actually was, and thus thought he didn't have to do the preparatory work. An embarrassingly large number of people jump to the same conclusion, and go rushing off to find the most powerful magic they can, serenely convinced of their own omnipotence. and proceed to burn out in one way or another.

Thus I do not recommend the practice of Enochian magic, and none of my books include instructions in it. That's not because I think nobody should do it; that's because I think that as long as you're dependent on instructional books to guide you in magical practice -- and it takes a decade or more of hard work to get past that stage -- you have no business messing with it. Once you've got sufficient mastery of the Great Art that you can tackle a system without benefit of handholding, why, it's up to you what you want to do.

comments.title: Tarot-Based Combinatorial System
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I'd like to try out the Tarot-based combinatorial system you outlined in the afterword to On the Shadows of the Ideas. I have the concepts & questions memorized, I'm just wondering how to get started now.

If I understand correctly, one rotates the the questions around concepts, so, say for subject x, if one was on G, Cooperation, the questions might be like:

Does x cooperate with other things?
What does x cooperate with?
In what contexts does x cooperate with these things?
From what do these cooperations originate?
Towards what are these cooperations moving?
(etc.)

So, after moving through all the questions for all the concepts, one would have gone through 484 questions (22x22)? I guess one advantage to a geomancy-based system would be that the process ends up being about half as many questions, though a system based on the I Ching's hexagrams would be about 10x as many questions!

Also, do you have sample topics to try this system out with? I'm wondering if one would use this system for anything one was interested in thinking about, or are certain types of topics more suited for it? Would it be better for something that Socrates might have discoursed about, like the concept of Justice or something, or, for instance, I'm writing a presentation on compost heat recovery systems right now, would that be a topic that one might use a combinatorial system to think over?

Anyways, great book by the way! I recommended it to a person who has a art of memory podcast that I follow, he was blown away by the quality of the publication, and I know a number of the students taking his memory course picked up a copy for their studies.

replies.title: Re: Tarot-Based Combinatorial System
replies.content:
It's best to use topics that are of interest to you, and figure out from experience which ones seem to work best with the system. Yes, you understand correctly how it works: taking each concept in turn, you ask each question of it. Taking compost as your first theme, for example, you ask AA: whether it is? AB: what is it? AC: in what is it? and so on, all the way to ZZ, what follows from the consequences? By the time you've thought through all these questions and concepts, there won't be much about compost you don't know!

If I was using the I Ching, by the way, I'd use eight concepts and eight questions, so as to have sixty-four combinations. I've used a seven-by-seven system with very good effect, so a relatively small set can still be quite effective.

Thanks for your kind words about the book. I'm very pleased with the way it came out.

comments.title: Chanting the Psalms
comments.content:
Do you see any problems with the practice of chanting the psalms while going through Learning Ritual Magic?

Not combining them in anyway, but doing them at different times in the day? I like the habit of chanting first thing when I wake up and before going to sleep.

replies.title: Re: Chanting the Psalms
replies.content: None at all. If you're comfortable enough with the Judeo-Christian tradition to invoke Hebrew names of God, you should get on well with the Psalms.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I finally gave into curiousity and plugged my birth data into the astrology chart website you recommend. I've always heard that April 19 is Aries, but the chart I got says Taurus, just barely-0 Tau 19'25". So that's interesting, and I guess I've been reading the wrong half-inch in yesterday's paper for giggles all my life.

The write up on the site about how to read it is pretty confusing, though. It talks about the planet above the horizon, but also says in the ascendant, which Neptune is in the ascendan, Capricorn, but below the horizon; Uranus, in Scorpio, is the first above the horizon.
Then there are no planets at all in three houses: Capricorn, Aquarius, and Picies, which are 12th, 11th, and 10th houses.

I suppose I need to get a book. What I can make out of it seems to fit with the various personality tests I've done: Meyers-Briggs and Enneagram.

BoysMom

P.S. The knife under the router seemed to work the first day, for disrupting the internet service, but alas, ceased to have an effect after that. It is directly under and against the router, with the point towards the connection to the cable to the internet. I'm thinking of trying a needle or perhaps a nail, directly under the hard drive of the computers, or maybe the monitors. Would be interested in your opinion as to which, as it is your experiment.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
A book would be a very good thing. I recommend, in fact, checking out books from the library one at a time, and looking up the things in your chart in each of them, using that as a framework for your astrological studies. Since you know yourself better than you know anyone else, it's possible to learn to make sense of astrology very quickly that way.

Since we're just beginning the experiment with the effect of iron on TV and the internet, I have no idea yet which of these would work! One thing, though -- don't give up too soon. It may take a while for a long-term effect to be noticed.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Oh, don't worry, I'm not giving up! I just wondered if you had a preference as to what was tested next.

One of the uphill problems here is my husband has PTSD and part of his screen addiction is as surely self-medication as anything else. Beats alcohol or drugs, I guess. I'm hoping more to disconnect the kids, but he's using screen to avoid parenting, so I have active opposition. He's probably going to require something more like a relationship explosion, unfortunately, and the untreated PTSD may well end up being the hill our marriage dies on.

BoysMom

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I'm sorry to hear that! It's worth keeping in mind, though, that glamour is only one of the things involved in television's obsessive power. People can have their own reasons for running away from life into a glass screen...

comments.title: Recommendations for a structured approach to ancient magic
comments.content:
Hi John Michael! I am asking on behalf of a small study group. We read and worked through the Picatrix last year. This year we are approaching the PGM (Betz) and Skinner's Techniques of Graeco-Egyptian Magic. Although Skinner's book is immensely helpful in getting situated in the papyri, it's not directly geared toward beginning practical work. I'm wondering if you know of any classes or books that boil down a structured approach to this system of magic. I have learned so much from your books and I appreciate your practical approach, such as, "Do this meditation for two weeks and then proceed to the next chapter." I'd love it if there was that kind of updated and systematized approach to ancient magic and am hoping you are familiar with something along those lines. Patrick Dunn and Stephen Flowers both have written some updated versions of a few of the operations, but I was hoping for a more structured system than what I found in either of those. Thank you! --Joy Vernon

P.S. I pre-ordered the anthology of astrological magic that is coming out from Three Hands Press this month and am very interested to see what's in there!

replies.title: Re: Recommendations for a structured approach to ancient magic
replies.content: I don't know of anything in print or online that presents a structured approach to classical Pagan magic. Your group is heading into unmarked territory! My suggestion would be to experiment, and have your group try to work out such a structured program itself, then publish it for the benefit of others.

replies.title: Re: Recommendations for a structured approach to ancient magic
replies.content: Hehe. Yes, we've discussed that! Both with the Picatrix (a motley mess and all out of order) and with this. I'll let you know if we come up with anything! Thank you!

replies.title: Re: Recommendations for a structured approach to ancient magic
replies.content: Just remember that the Picatrix is a deliberately motley mess. The author knew exactly what he was doing, and jumbled things up to force the student to learn the principles, and then work from there to an understanding of the practice.

comments.title: Calling of the Elements
comments.content:
I have been making a few tweaks to the SOP ritual so that it feels more personal. One of these has been to request specific types of blessings from the various elements. For example, when calling Air, I ask for clear thinking. These requests have changed bit by bit over time, and it has had the benefit of requiring a lot of thought about what each of the elements means.

I wondered what your take is on this practice—would you say it is better to leave it as a more open-ended blessing rather than get too detailed?

I have tried it both ways, and for me, asking for specific aspects of the elemental blessings helps me feel more deeply engaged in the ritual.

replies.title: Re: Calling of the Elements
replies.content: Specific requests are entirely appropriate. By all means continue with it.

comments.title: Secret Fire
comments.content:
I have seen a couple of references to the Telluric Current as the ‘secret fire’. This is puzzling to me, because I mostly associate the Telluric Current with the earth. Why would an earth-based force be symbolized as fire?
Typically earth is considered to be a female element, and fire is a masculine element. Is it because the Telluric Current is the energy emanating from the earth, and not the earth itself?

replies.title: Re: Secret Fire
replies.content:
If you were a student of mine, I'd instruct you to meditate on the telluric current as a secret fire until you understood why that phrase is accurate. As you're not, I'll simply point out that the map is not the territory, and the universe doesn't pay a lot of attention to our notions about what this or that should be. There is a subtle energy within the earth; we can call it "the secret fire," because that phrase symbolizes some aspects of it; we can also call it something else -- and it has a vast number of names -- but those names don't tell it what to be, they just give us handles by which we can understand a little of it.

You'll get a lot more out of occultism if, the next time you see something that doesn't seem to make sense, you stop and say to yourself, "This is trying to teach me something I don't know yet." In what ways does the earth contain a hidden fire? Think!

replies.title: Re: Secret Fire
replies.content:
From Goats and Roses

Or not so secret fire! Look at some of the videos of Kilauea right now.

replies.title: Re: Secret Fire
replies.content: Thanks JMG. I have a number of similar questions which I will continue to mull over and see if I can work out the meanings. I appreciate the opportunity to ask these questions here!

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi AD Greer,

I'm currently reading through your book _The Druid Magic Handbook_, while also continuing to practice the Dolmen Arch course. I’d like to do the work of the DMH alongside the DA, but before I go any further I wanted to check to make sure that these two systems are compatible enough to work on together.

Also, I have some questions about setting intentions, particularly in how to use symbols for this purpose. Is it generally more effective to use symbols, or are there cases when intentions would be better stated directly?

For example, I often set intentions just for self-improvement. So I might, say, set an intention to be more compassionate in my life. In this case, the intention seems simple enough and could be stated directly, such as, "I intend to be compassionate toward myself and others today and always." However, it does seem rather difficult to concentrate on the idea of compassion. Do you have any thoughts on the most effective way to set these kinds of intentions?

As always, thank you for your time!

Kelly T.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
The Druid Magic Handbook and the Dolmen Arch course are entirely compatible with one another, since they use the same symbolism and ritual forms; you can combine them freely. (I certainly did!)

I had to go look up "setting intentions," as it's not something I've practiced. I'd tend to think that just focusing on an abstract idea such as compassion wouldn't do much, as that's not going to sink below the conscious mind. What I'd do in that case is to find places in my life where my thoughts, words, and actions lack compassion, and systematically practice being more compassionate in those places. Every evening I'd review the day and look for ways I could have been more compassionate, make plans accordingly, and then carry out those plans. That's less magic than Stoic philosophy, but it does work...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ah, yes. I've been reading some of the writings of the Stoics recently, so that makes sense. I'm also reminded of what Manly Hall says in _Self-Unfoldment_, that the best way to correct negative behaviors is to just stop acting that way. I laughed heartily when I read that!

As for my question about setting intentions, I was just thinking about your discussion in the DMH of the principle of intentionality, as well as the end of the full DA version of the SOP (when you state your intention and send it out into the cosmos).

Given what you've said here, I guess I'm wondering what kinds of intentions could be used as part of my daily SOP practice. I thought that the suggested intention in the DA materials (about individuality) was similar to the idea of awakening compassion, but perhaps they're different. Would it be more appropriate, then, to continue to use the DA's suggested intention since it would ultimately help me with carry out my plans via Stoic philosophy?

Many thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Hall was a very clever guy.

By all means try different requests with the Sphere of Protection, and see what results you get.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Do you have any thoughts on art and craft as means of working magic? Like, writing, painting, knitting, pottery, to name a few examples?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's mostly unknown territory in modern ceremonial magic. If you want to pursue it, get a good working knowledge of at least one system of magic, and then see what you can come up with.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you :)

comments.title: Experiment
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I decided immediately upon reading your May 8th entry to take part in the experiment. I put a 20cm sharp knife under my router I think the next day. Haven't experienced any noticeable effects so far, but I'm patient. I just wanted to make sure I'm doing this properly, so here's a couple questions:

Neither the router, nor the knife under it, which is lengthwise is a tad wider, are particularly well hidden- they're just behind the flat screen on top of the desk, but not aligned so at to remain hidden when I'm sitting here being glamoured -or glamouring myself, perhaps. should I try to hide them more effectively? One thing to note, though, is that this powerfully hypnotizing technology being what it is, I don't often notice -or, alas, think- much of anything outside it when I'm under it's influence. I don't know if it's happened to you or you've been told about it, but it's the most common thing to get up from the desktop and be violently struck with an urge to use the restroom.

Second question - I'm not using a foldable knife, but a 20cm vegetable-cutting -if that's a word?- knife: is this important? I brought home years ago a pocket knife like the one you used in the thumbnail to the May 8th entry, but it's never been really mine since I found it on the street, I've never used it, and is of course quite blunt. Should I sharpen it and use it instead?

Finally, regarding the incantation, I should just utter it while I'm placing the knife under the router, and live it at that?

Thanks for all you do, and all the best

Lorenzo

replies.title: Re: Experiment
replies.content: Nobody knows yet what does and doesn't work; all I have to go on is a few reports from people who've tried it. The one thing that I do know is that, according to the traditional lore, the knife needs to have a very sharp point, so if your knife is dull, you won't get any effect. Beyond that, every data point that comes in is a new discovery!

Mars is in the first house in Cancer conjunct the second house cusp; Pluto is tightly conjunct with Saturn in Libra in 5. Except the time of birth is a bit fuzzy; it's possible that Saturn and Pluto should be in 4.

The thing is... I know your take on Pluto. (I was born in 1983, so it should still have some relevance.) And I know your answer is probably that you'd look it up in a good book of astrology, of which I have several. But I always take my dreams seriously when they even touch on magic or the spiritual, so... here we are. Any thoughts? If not, no worries.

-Steve T

replies.title: Re: Pluto square Mars
replies.content: I'd advise you to look it up in a good book on astrology. ;-)

comments.title: Auric Goggles
comments.content:
Greetings, JMG.

I've recently learned about the auric goggles developed from the research of Dr. Walter Kilner in the 1930's.

If you're not familiar, they are used to allow one to see auras. As far as I understand, they filter out nearly all of the visible spectrum, only allowing UV through. After wearing them for a time, one's eyes become sensitized to UV light, and so after taking the goggles off one is supposedly able to see auras.

What information I've found so far on line is pretty clearly jumbled up with quite a bit of conspiracy theory nonsense, and so I wanted to ask you:

What's your take on the goggles? Do you think they work? And if so, can you elaborate on the process/theory of how they work?

Thanks!

replies.title: Re: Auric Goggles
replies.content: The famous dicyanin Kilner goggles! Back when I was doing radionics, I tried to find a source for dicyanin filters, but wasn't able to get anything of the kind. That being the case, I have no idea how or whether they work -- but I'd still like a pair sometime, if only as a classic occult tchotchke.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
I know solar and telluric energy have to be balanced, what other sorts of things have to be balanced in a magic system?

Some of the enlightenment-seeking traditions regard the siddhis (psychic and magic abilities that manifest as a byproduct of spiritual practices) as a distraction from the goal of enlightenment. They ignore and dismiss these abilities. There may be some who embrace the siddhis but I've seen this rejection from multiple sources. I know you don't practice these methods yourself but from your point of view, is that the right thing for those paths to do, or does it reject something potentially good and useful and risk unbalancing their systems?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
It depends on the magical system...

Enlightenment-seeking systems, by definition, focus all their efforts on attaining enlightenment, so of course they're going to discourage anything that will distract you from the quest for enlightenment, for the same reason that a ballet school will discourage anything that will distract its students from practicing ballet! If your goal is to attain enlightenment, following their advice is a good idea. If you have other goals -- and there are a great many other worthwhile goals you can have -- why, you need to follow different advice.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Greetings, JMG.

1. About myths. Is there a creation myth in modern Druidry or in the old Celtic religion? And what of the supreme being? Celi the Hidden One, Spirit of Life, comes to mind. But I do not recall any creation myths.

2. About reincarnation. So, if human souls can transmigrate into animal bodies and we are going to have a decrease in human population in the future (and supposedly an increase in animal population), are we going to have a lot of very clever animals or will these souls be stuck in a kind of "limbo", neither able to incarnate nor move on to luminous life?

3. Continuing with the theme, practicing ritual magic is not the only way from Abred to Gwynfydd, is it?

P.S. the Mount of Fir-Trees sounds like a lovely place to be in. Is it a real place or...an idea?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) There's a very fragmentary creation myth in Iolo Morganwg's Barddas, but that's about it. As for supreme beings, here again, Iolo talks about one, whose hidden name is the Secret of the Bards of the Island of Britain, but the usual Abrahamic notion of God the Creator isn't particularly common outside Middle Eastern desert religions and their offspring.

2) Well, we have a lot of impressively stupid humans these days, so I think it's considerably more likely that we'll have a lot of perfectly ordinary, not especially bright animals with dim half-conscious memories of walking on two legs and voting for either Clinton or Trump. ;-)

3) Of course not. It's one of many paths.

Mons Abiegnus, the Mount of Fir-Trees, is the symbolic Rosicrucian mountain of initiation, the Mons Magorum Invisibilis (Invisible Mount of the Mages) in Vaughan's famous engraving:
[IMAGE:0]

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What exactly does a binding do as a magical working? What does it "bind" the target to? what does that mean?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's like asking "what does a computer program do?" It depends on the program, and similarly, it depends on the binding. You choose what limit you want to put on a target, and put that explicitly into the ritual. For example, many years ago, I had a friend who was trying to get away from an abusive boyfriend; at her request, I did a binding spell, binding him from hurting her. He went away and never bothered her again. That's an example of a successful binding.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That sounds like a very useful spell. Is it something a beginner can do?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nope. You'll want three to five years of daily practice under your belt before you want to try something like that.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: Was phrenology connected to occult methods or philosophy?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Nope. It was based on an inaccurate idea of the location of personality traits in the brain.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

After trying the vinegar in saucer - bedroom tip, and the cold iron - TV / router experiment, both to immediate positive effect, I have bought and started working with your book Encyclopedia of Natural Magic.

Some further steps I have taken here is that I have ordered a moss agate pendant, set in silver, to be used with the intention of 'heal the land--starting with my garden'. How is the magical timing used in this circumstance?

Also, what is the relationship between the planetary intelligences and the gods of the same name? are they the same?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Moss agate is a stone of Mercury. Every Wednesday (the day of Mercury), during one of the planetary hours of Mercury (look up "planetary hours" in a search engine and you'll find plenty of tables and calculators to find these), burn an incense of Mercury, hold the pendant in the smoke, and concentrate on your intention. Then put on the pendant, and make sure you do at least one thing in the following seven days (best of all, on that day) to improve the condition of your garden.

Intelligences are of many levels and kinds. The planetary intelligences contacted by ceremonial magicians are a good deal lower on the Great Chain of Being than the planetary gods are; the intelligences serve the gods, and you can invoke the god of a planet as part of a spell to call on the influence of the intelligence of that planet.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you!

comments.title: Prayer
comments.content: You mention prayer quite a bit, I'm curious if you could point me to a book or blog post where you've gone into more detail about prayer. I grew up in the suburbs, with watered-down lutheranism as my only example of prayer. Basically people seemed to just close their eyes, ask god for something and mumble amen and that was about it. I'm thinking that in the occult world there's more too it than that?

replies.title: Re: Prayer
replies.content: There's a huge amount more to it than that. I haven't written extensively about prayer, but there's a chapter in my first book, Paths of Wisdom, that discusses it.

comments.title: seeing auras around shadows
comments.content:
(Deborah Bender)

When I'm paying attention, I can see auras around people and trees as a colorless, featureless glow of variable size and intensity, resembling a heat wave over pavement on a hot day. I have a clairvoyant friend who can see colors, layers and other details in a human aura, but I can't.

I am curious because I see a similar aura around the edges of shadows cast on a sidewalk on sunny days. The "aura" around a person's shadow resembles the aura around a person's body in that it is larger and brighter around the head. But I also see auras around the shadows of inanimate objects. I don't have to be in any special mental state other than paying attention to what I'm looking at to see them. Nobody else I have talked to seems to see auras around shadows on sidewalks. So I am wondering whether this has a purely physical explanation like maybe I am seeing infrared radiation that other people don't see, or something.

replies.title: Re: seeing auras around shadows
replies.content: Interesting! I'm not at all sure what that means.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hello John,
I have tried to break our habit of staring at screens. I taped Xacto blades to the bottom of the TV and the router, and slipped needles into the cases on my and my wife’s phones. I hope that will bring down the level of distress in the house. I have strayed from the path of the Facebook in the last weeks, even before the sharps were added to the path.
That’s not why I write today. Our son, who lives in Arizona, does not wish to have any contact with us. I occasionally send him an Email with family news, and he communicates from time to time with his sister (who still lives with us). I am resigned to living with it, while hoping that he will one day change his mind. My wife is unable to accept that she cannot force him to contact us. I have tried to convince her to accept with “logical” reasoning to no avail.
Can you suggest anything which I can do which lead her to the paths of acceptance?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You can't make anyone else accept something they don't want to accept. Mind you, by the same token, I can't make you accept that!

As you assess your habit of staring at screens, you might want to think about what role the screens play in your life, and in particular what they help you avoid dealing with. Glamour isn't the only factor that leads people to stare at jerky little pictures on glass screens...

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
Hi JMG.

I noticed something interesting this past Friday when I was at my cousin's wedding. During one particular prayer, when the minister was blessing the minutes-from-being-wed couple, I visualized (wholly unbidden and unintentionally) a faint, shimmering shaft of light descending from the heavens and being redirected from my hands (outstretched and together in the traditional prayer position). In your estimation, was this a mystical experience aided by the rituals of the wedding done in a presumably consecrated chapel? It certainly felt like one.

Also, it's interesting that you mentioned the etheric energies at sporting events, because when you think about it - many sports fans (not to mention the athletes themselves) are quite superstitious, even when they are of professions and backgrounds that "mean" they "should know better" (read: "should ignore better so that I as a staunch scientific materialist need not think uncomfortable thoughts about my worldview"). And, of course, the whole process of prompted cheering is in and of itself highly ritualistic.

Hmm... I anticipate going to a baseball game in about three weeks, so I might have a datum then.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
I'd call it an astral experience rather than a mystical one, but it's still worth paying attention to. Do you have such experiences often?

As for sports, my Aspergers syndrome keeps me from sharing in the astral and etheric communion that those highly ritualized events are designed to induce, but one consequence is that back when I attended them, I noticed things that other people often didn't. The fans and athletes aren't superstitious at all -- they know perfectly well what's going on, even if they don't have the technical vocabulary to talk about it clearly.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Actually, this was the first time I've noticed something like that (at least in a while). However, Uranus has recently ingressed into my 4th house (not sure if that would affect anything), and I was less than 12 hours away from a Lunar Return (I *can* see how *that* could affect it).

And this is also the first wedding I can recall attending that occurred on a Friday (which, as a Venus day, would seem exceptionally well suited to that sort of thing).

Thanks, I really appreciate all the work you've done.
-Anthony

replies.title: Re: Library usage of books?
replies.content: I also take notes from library books, so that should be okay to begin with. You'll find that having your own copy is much more convenient, though. ;-)

comments.title: SoP
comments.content:
Hi JM,

I hope all is well with you.

You answered some questions for me last week, and thank you for those. Prior to then I had been performing it ad hoc, whenever convenient, and was finding my ability to do it every day slipping.

Since last Monday, I've been practicing the SoP as I get out of bed (typically before dawn) and I think the consistent timing helps. I've completed chapter 4, and am looking to revise and refine all the active and receptive workings for each element and spirit.

I'm also practicing the divination away from my banished space (in the kitchen, as I prepare breakfast), and it's probably too low resolution data to draw conclusive inferences but I've noticed I'm drawing Ogham fews I hadn't drawn so often previously. A cursory check of my journal indicates there may be a pattern but this may just be confirmation bias.

So for today, I have a couple of questions if you may.

1). When drawing the elements, do you position them opposite your face or solar plexus, and does it matter if they are perfectly symmetrical or not?

2). I am having some difficulty with consistent visuals - I can do water very easily (I think I have an affinity for elemental water). I struggle with visuals for fire, particularly. I live in a place with some high temperatures at times and I have plenty of memory source material but I find it hard to make it stick. Any insight that way, or any I just projecting my biases?

The meditation is going better in so far as I'm able to apply myself a bit better during the quieter, early hours.

Thanks again, and much appreciated

replies.title: Re: SoP
replies.content:
Delighted to hear it.

1) I normally trace them more or less opposite shoulder level, because that's where my hand ends up naturally when I extend it. It shouldn't matter that much, though.

2) Everyone has problems with consistent visuals, especially for the first decade or so of magical practice. ;-) Stick with it, and it'll get better.

replies.title: Re: SoP
replies.content:
Thank you.

Do the gates need be perfectly formed, and shaped, or does a rough trade of the shape bear the same results? Just curious if precision of gesture is more beneficial

replies.title: Re: SoP
replies.content: Experiment and find out!

comments.title: Amulet for Elemental balance
comments.content:
I have been told by a few astrologers that I have very little water element on my natal chart, and a large prevalence of earth element.

After reading your Encyclopwdia of Natural Magic, I though I'd try and making an amulet for elemental balance.

I made a necklace of sardonyx beads with a beryl pendant positioned to sit at the solar plexus height.

I've word it for the first time today and felt a very strong downward energy, also mental fog and irritability. The first two seem to correlate with water effects countering the rising energy of fire, however the irritability was somewhat unexpected. I had to take it off after a few hours. I also had some difficulty breathing and I wonder it could be an issue of positioning water in front of the solar plexus.

Did I get the elemental correspondence wrong? Should I balance it out with some fire influence?

Thank you kindly.

replies.title: Re: Amulet for Elemental balance
replies.content: No, you got the elemental correspondence right but your basic theory was wrong. If you don't have much water in your natal chart, not having much water is natural for you. You can't change that, and you don't gain anything by trying to force yourself into an arbitrary state of balance. For you, "balance" means just a bit of water and a lot of earth. I'd encourage you to give the necklace as a gift to someone who has a lot of water in her natal chart -- she'll find it very comfortable.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: What is it about incense that gives it magical properties? Would boiling the same herbs and spices found in a particular incense do anything magically?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: If you boil it, you make a potion, which can be very useful in other applications. You use incense to charge the air with a magical influence.

comments.title: mysticism
comments.content:
Hi JMG,
Forgive me for asking a question you've answered a few times already, but I cannot find where I wrote the title down, if indeed I did. Can you remind me of the author and title of the book you recommend on mysticism? It might even be simply named that, but perhaps not. All I recall is that the author is female and that you pointed readers to it when they enquired into how mysticism and ritual magic differ.

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: mysticism
replies.content: That would be Mysticism by Evelyn Underhill. She was a Golden Dawnie and a good friend of Arthur Edward Waite, and her book is well worth reading even if you don't have an interest in mysticism as such.

replies.title: Re: mysticism
replies.content: Wonderful! Thank you. Details now written in the right spot in the right notebook for later reference.

comments.title: Fourfold Breathing & Color Breathing
comments.content:
For my regular mediation practice, I have been doing 5 minutes of Fourfold Breath followed by 10 minutes of discursive meditation. I had started out doing color breathing before discursive meditation because I found the color breathing easier and fourfold breath too difficult. Once I got the hang of the fourfold breathing, I dropped the color breathing. Are the two interchangeable breathing exercises, or should I ideally be doing both?

By the way, I want to thank you for introducing me to discursive meditation through the Druidry Handbook. I had tried Eastern-based meditation practices before but was unable to keep them up. Even though I am still a beginner, I have found a lot of value in the discursive methods.

replies.title: Re: Fourfold Breathing & Color Breathing
replies.content:
You can do either one. These days I mostly do the fourfold breath.

You're welcome and thank you, by the way. I also came to discursive meditation after a lot of experience with Asian methods, and found it much more suited to my needs.

comments.title: Duped Mystics/Mages
comments.content: Hi, JMG. I have noticed from time to time you have mentioned (or quoted) Manly P. Hall. I have not read any of his works but I have read about him. One thing that struck me is that in his senior years he fell prey to con-men. Granted that mages and mystics are still human, I have been somewhat surprised that a person who possesses much wisdom can be duped so thoroughly. Seems a dreadful pity! I would like to know from your perspective how, and why, such a thing happens to some mages and/or mystics.

replies.title: Re: Duped Mystics/Mages
replies.content: Late in life, Hall convinced himself that he didn't need to keep meditating or engaging in other spiritual practices, and so his wisdom became a matter of outward knowledge (backed up by an immense amount of kindness) rather than living insight. The moral to this story is that you should plan on keeping up your daily practices right up to your last day on this side of the grass!

comments.title: Spring mishaps
comments.content: Thank you for hosting Magic Monday. My daughter seems to have an unusually high proportion of mishaps or illnesses around late spring - April and particularly May. How might I go about discovering if there is an occult connection, and if so, some way of restoring whatever's gone awry?

replies.title: Re: Spring mishaps
replies.content: I'd ask a licensed health care practitioner to look for spring allergies that cause mild mental confusion. That's much more likely than anything occult.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content: On one of the old blogs, you recommended a book on the history of the astonishingly many quirky spiritual movements that have gotten started in Upstate New York/the Hudson Valley. I've sadly lost the reference; would you kindly remind me of the title and author?  Thanks!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I don't recall reading such a book, and would like to do so. Can anyone suggest such a book?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Occult America by Mitch Horowitz discusses some of the spiritual movements that started in New York's Hudson Valley and Burned-Over Districts.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I’ve read Occult America.  It’s a good book.  I’m sorry to report there is nothing on American Druidry, though.  Maybe John will rate a chapter in the sequel!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I first discovered the Burnt-Over District in Karen Armstrong's A History of God. It's only a very small part of the book but it's linked to a discussion of how intense 'revival' experiences tend to burn out after a couple of years, compared to slow and steady spiritual development.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This is not at all what you asked about, but I can recommend another very interesting book about the area: Judith Richardson's Possessions: The History and Uses of Haunting in the Hudson Valley.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Could this have been Michael Barkun's 1986 Crucible of the Millenium: The Burnt-Over District of New York in the 1840s? I don't remember it mentioned on the blog, but it's in the bibliography of JMG's very worthwile Apocalypse: A History of the End of Time.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
An update on the subject of unexpected results of applying cold iron to electronics: I haven't seen any actual reduction in the amount of TV/internet time since I needled my laptop and my family's router/TV - but my parents *did* go out and buy a second pair of headphones the next day so that I wouldn't be disturbed by the noise when they watch TV, and I did get around to uninstalling an excessively time-wasting computer game.

(The headphones makes sense. I suspect the biggest reason for my own internet usage is my strong alienation from the local culture; I wouldn't be surprised if the same applies to my parents with a possible side of glamoring over being unable to contribute to Progress. Moreover, the proximate cause for my irritation was that the noise made it difficult to concentrate, so I rather think I did get my intention...)
